claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 9 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 5 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 9 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 5 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 9 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Blake
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 5 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'll help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! To get started, I need to ask you some questions about your child (or children).

**First, let's start with the child's age:**

The eligibility requirements vary depending on the child's age. Could you tell me:
- How old is your child?
- If you're asking about multiple children, please let me know each of their ages

The general age categories are:
- **Under 16**: May automatically qualify
- **16-19**: May qualify if in approved education or training
- **20 or over**: Generally not eligible

What is your child's age?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi! Yes, I have two children I'd like to ask about. Alex is 9 years old and Blake is 5 years old. They both live with me here in the UK.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! Let me assess the eligibility for both Alex (9 years old) and Blake (5 years old).

Since both children are under 16, they fall into the category that may automatically qualify. Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! Now, the next check is about employment:

**Are Alex and Blake working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work at all)?**

Since they're both under 16 (9 and 5 years old), I expect they're not in paid work, but I need to confirm this to continue the assessment.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Oh no, they're not working at all! They're just kids - Alex is in primary school and Blake is only 5, so they're both just at school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect, that's what I expected! Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Next question:

**Are Alex and Blake NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right?**

In other words, are they receiving benefits like Employment and Support Allowance (ESA) or Universal Credit in their own name? 

For children aged 9 and 5, this would be very unusual, but I need to confirm they're not receiving these benefits to continue.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, they don't receive any benefits in their own names. They're just children - they don't claim anything themselves.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Let me continue with the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Next question:

**Are Alex and Blake in any apprenticeships?**

This includes:
- English apprenticeships (Intermediate, Advanced, Higher, or Degree Apprenticeships)
- Scottish Modern Apprenticeships
- Welsh Apprenticeships (except Foundation level, which are approved)

Again, since they're 9 and 5 years old and in primary school, I expect the answer is no, but I need to confirm this.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, definitely not! They're far too young for apprenticeships - they're just in primary school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Let me move forward with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now I need to check your residency status.

**Do you meet the UK residency requirements for Child Benefit?**

You mentioned you and the children live in the UK. To qualify, you need to meet at least ONE of these conditions:

1. **You normally live in the UK** (the most common situation)
2. You have settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme
3. You have pre-settled status AND are working/studying/job-seeking, etc.
4. You're living abroad as a Crown servant

Since you mentioned you live in the UK, do you **normally live in the UK**? This means the UK is your main home where you usually reside.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's right - the UK is our main home. We live here normally.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! Now let's check responsibility for the children.

**Are you responsible for Alex and Blake?**

You've already mentioned that both children live with you in the UK, which means you meet the responsibility requirement. Just to confirm:

Do **both Alex (9) and Blake (5) live with you** as their main home?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's correct - both Alex and Blake live with me at home.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Let me continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good! Now I need to check about other potential claimants.

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex or Blake?**

For example:
- Another parent or guardian
- A former partner
- Another family member

This is important because only one person can claim Child Benefit for each child at a time.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for them. It's just me looking after them.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now I need to check about the children's living situation.

**Are Alex and Blake currently:**
- In hospital?
- In local authority care?
- Being fostered?
- Or are they living normally with you at home?

From what you've told me, it sounds like they're living normally with you at home. Is that correct?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's right - they're both living normally at home with me. They're not in hospital, care, or being fostered.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Let me continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Almost there! One final question:

**Is the local council NOT paying towards Alex and Blake's accommodation or maintenance?**

In other words, are you responsible for their living costs yourself (not the local council paying for them)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's correct - I'm responsible for all their living costs myself. The council isn't paying anything towards their accommodation or maintenance.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
# 🎉 Great News! You Are Eligible for Child Benefit!

**Result: ELIGIBLE for both Alex and Blake**

You meet all the Child Benefit eligibility requirements for both of your children:

✅ **Alex (9 years old)** - Fully eligible  
✅ **Blake (5 years old)** - Fully eligible

---

## Next Steps to Claim:

1. **Apply online or by post** using form CH2
2. **Provide proof of identity** for yourself and both children
3. **Provide birth certificates** or adoption certificates for Alex and Blake

---

## Important Information:

💷 **High Income Child Benefit Charge:**  
If you or your partner earn over £60,000 per year, you may need to pay the High Income Child Benefit Charge through Self Assessment. However, you can still claim Child Benefit - many people do so to protect their National Insurance credits even if they choose not to receive the payments.

💰 **Payment Schedule:**  
- Payments are usually made every 4 weeks
- Or weekly if you're a single parent or receiving certain benefits

---

## How to Apply:

You can find all the information you need to apply here:  
🔗 [https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim](https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim)

Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit or the application process?

reasonings: Alex is 9 years old (under 16), not in paid work, not receiving benefits in their own right, not in an apprenticeship, lives with the claimant in the UK, no one else is claiming for them, living normally at home, and the council is not paying towards their accommodation or maintenance. All eligibility criteria are met.

Blake is 5 years old (under 16), not in paid work, not receiving benefits in their own right, not in an apprenticeship, lives with the claimant in the UK, no one else is claiming for them, living normally at home, and the council is not paying towards their accommodation or maintenance. All eligibility criteria are met.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for both children (Alex, 9, and Blake, 5). Both children meet all the qualifying criteria: they are under 16, not working, not receiving benefits in their own right, not in apprenticeships, living with the claimant who normally resides in the UK, has responsibility for them, no other claimant exists, they are living normally at home, and the council is not paying for their care.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.question: Are you responsible for the child?
response.help_text: Responsibility means living with child OR contributing to their upkeep

criteria: You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care
Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

paths.description: Paying maintenance under court order or agreement
criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to child location checks

response.description: Check if another person is claiming Child Benefit for this child
response.routing_logic: If no other claimant, proceed. If other claimant exists and parties agree who should claim, route accordingly. If dispute exists, defer to HMRC for arbitration.

response.description: Determine if special rules apply based on child's location
response.routing_logic: Check if child is in hospital, care, fostered, or living normally

response.question: Is the local council NOT paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance?
response.help_text: You generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child

response.description: Applicant meets all criteria for Child Benefit
response.reason: Meets all Child Benefit eligibility requirements
